TL;DR: This paper introduces SEST, a transformer-based model for predicting saliency from event camera data. It addresses the lack of datasets and strong baselines by introducing new benchmarks and leveraging event-native pretraining with synthetic supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the growing interest in saliency prediction in RGB images/videos, event-based saliency prediction remains underexplored due to the lack of datasets and robust baselines.

Method: The authors propose SEST, a Swin Transformer-based model pretrained using event-native, self-supervised learning. It combines this pretrained backbone with a CNN decoder for saliency map generation.

Result: SEST outperforms existing models for event-based saliency prediction, narrows the gap with RGB-based methods, and demonstrates transferability to real event camera data in zero-shot evaluations.

Conclusion: This work pioneers the application of deep learning to event-based saliency prediction, introducing novel datasets and a strong baseline model, thereby charting a new pathway in event-based vision research.

Abstract: Saliency prediction has been extensively studied in RGB images and videos as a computational model of human visual attention. In contrast, predicting saliency from event-based data remains largely unexplored, despite the biological inspiration and favorable sensing properties of event cameras. Two obstacles have held this direction back: the absence of large-scale event saliency datasets, and the lack of a strong baseline. In this paper, we introduce SEST (Swin Event-based Saliency Transformer), a transformer-based model for saliency prediction from event data, bridging the data scarcity barrier through event-native pretraining and synthetic supervision. SEST leverages a self-supervised pretrained event-based Swin Transformer backbone combined with a lightweight CNN decoder to produce dynamic saliency maps. To address the scarcity of annotated event-based saliency data, we introduce two new benchmark datasets, N-DHF1K and N-UCF Sports, generated from large-scale RGB saliency benchmarks. Experimental results show that SEST clearly outperforms existing event-based saliency methods and narrows the performance gap with state-of-the-art RGB models. Zero-shot evaluation on a real event camera dataset further demonstrates that our model trained on synthetic data remains transferable on real event streams. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to apply deep learning to event-based saliency prediction, opening a new research direction at the intersection of event-based vision and neuromorphic visual attention.

</details>


### [174] [Not Too Generative, Not Too Discriminative: The Human Alignment Sweet Spot](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23819)
*Jorge Chang Ortega,Bastien Le Lan,Thomas Serre,Victor Boutin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether human-like visual representations align better with discriminative or generative learning, finding that a hybrid approach yields stronger alignment with human vision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study seeks to determine if the alignment of human-like visual representations is better explained by discriminative or generative learning objectives and addresses confounding variables present in prior research.

Method: The researchers use Joint Energy-Based Models (JEMs) to create a continuum between discriminative and generative training within a fixed architecture, isolating the effect of the learning objective by varying a single mixing coefficient. The models are evaluated across six human-alignment benchmarks.

Result: The study finds that human alignment is consistently maximized at intermediate points in the generative-discriminative continuum, where hybrid JEMs demonstrate more human-like behavior across various levels of vision.

Conclusion: The results imply that the dichotomy between generative and discriminative objectives is not sufficient for understanding human-aligned vision. Instead, a balance between both objectives is key to achieving alignment.

Abstract: A central question in computational vision is whether human-like visual representations are better explained by discriminative or generative learning. Existing comparisons, however, often confound the learning objective with architecture, scale, and training data, leaving open whether the objective itself drives alignment. We address this confound using Joint Energy-Based Models (JEMs), which interpolate continuously between discriminative and generative training within a fixed architecture. By varying a single mixing coefficient, we isolate the effect of the learning objective and evaluate the resulting models across six human-alignment benchmarks spanning perceptual similarity, gloss perception, human response uncertainty, robustness, shape-texture cue conflict, and diagnostic feature attribution. Across this diverse suite, human alignment is consistently maximized at intermediate points of the generative-discriminative continuum, rather than at either endpoint. Hybrid JEMs combine the categorical structure induced by discriminative learning with the sensitivity to input structure induced by generative learning, yielding more human-like behavior across multiple levels of vision. These results suggest that the generative-discriminative dichotomy is the wrong axis for understanding human-aligned vision: alignment emerges not from choosing one objective over the other, but from balancing both.

</details>


### [175] [Decomposing Queries into Tool Calls for Long-Video Keyframe Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23826)
*Michal Shlapentokh-Rothman,Prachi Garg,Yu-Xiong Wang,Derek Hoiem*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ToolMerge is a method for keyframe retrieval for long-video QA, leveraging an LLM-based planner for query decomposition and ranking merging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for selecting keyframes in long-video QA either evaluate frames against a single query or use a fixed query decomposition schema, limiting flexibility and precision.

Method: ToolMerge uses a Large Language Model to decompose queries into tool-specific calls and merges their rankings using boolean operations. This approach is evaluated using the newly introduced M2M benchmark.

Result: ToolMerge is competitive in retrieval tasks and achieves a 5% improvement in caption retrieval compared to existing keyframe selection methods.

Conclusion: ToolMerge provides an innovative and effective approach for keyframe retrieval in long-video QA, offering performance improvements and adaptability.

Abstract: Keyframe selection is a direct way to provide verifiable visual evidence for long-video question answering (QA). Queries differ in what they require, and finding the right frames depends on knowing what to look for. Existing keyframe selectors either score every frame against a single query, or decompose the query into a fixed schema evaluated by a single visual tool. We propose ToolMerge, a keyframe retrieval method based on decomposition and merging: an Large Language Model (LLM) based planner decomposes the query into tool calls and specifies how their per-tool rankings are merged using boolean operators. To evaluate retrieval directly, we construct Molmo-2 Moments (M2M), a benchmark in which every question is anchored to a specific time interval by construction. Across QA, question retrieval, and caption retrieval, ToolMerge is competitive with prior keyframe selectors, most notably on caption retrieval, outperforming other methods by 5%. Code and data can be found at https://github.com/michalsr/ToolMerge .

</details>


### [176] [MuellerPT: Decomposition Driven Pretraining for Dense Learning in Mueller Polarimetry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23840)
*Adam Tlemsani,Yingdian Li,Maxime Giot,Naim Slim,Christopher J. Peters,Abhijeet Ghosh,Daniel S. Elson*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces MuellerPT, a physics-guided pre-training approach for learning transferable dense representations from Mueller matrices, demonstrating improved label efficiency and domain robustness in biomedical imaging tasks like segmentation and classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in biomedical tissue analysis from Mueller matrix imaging, including scarce dense annotations and strong domain shifts, and to enhance label efficiency and domain transferability.

Method: MuellerPT pre-trains an encoder by predicting Lu-Chipman decomposition maps from 4x4 Mueller matrices. The encoder is then fine-tuned for specific biomedical imaging tasks like segmentation and classification using the newly created Multispectral Animal Polarimetric Organ dataset (MAP-Org).

Result: For segmentation, MuellerPT achieved over 20% DICE score improvement using 5% of training data compared to models trained from scratch. For classification, it improved accuracy by 8% using just 1% of training data. It also demonstrated robustness to domain shifts in qualitative evaluations.

Conclusion: MuellerPT offers a practical and effective approach for label-efficient biomedical imaging with Mueller polarimetry, leveraging physics-guided pre-training to achieve significant performance gains and robustness across tasks and domains.

Abstract: Mueller matrix imaging provides rich, physically meaningful contrast for biomedical tissue analysis, but supervised learning is hindered by scarce dense annotations and strong domain shifts across specimens and acquisition settings. We introduce MuellerPT, a physics guided pre-training approach that learns transferable dense representations by predicting Lu-Chipman decomposition maps from per-pixel 4x4 Mueller matrices. To scale pre-training, we collected a new large Multispectral Animal Polarimetric Organ dataset (MAP-Org). The pre-trained encoder is adapted with a segmentation head for grey vs. white matter segmentation in lamb brain. A classification head is used for colorectal cancer vs. non-cancer classification. Both segmentation and classification are evaluated across few-shot learning scenarios. In segmentation, MuellerPT improves label efficiency and cross specimen transfer compared to models without pre-training, achieving an absolute DICE gain of over 20% compared to the baseline trained from scratch when using 5% of the training data. In classification, MuellerPT also enhances label efficiency, improving overall accuracy by 8% compared to the baseline when using 1% of the training data. We demonstrate MuellerPT's robustness to domain shift with a qualitative evaluation of its predicted Lu-Chipman maps on an ex vivo human oesophagus sample. These results suggest that predicting Lu-Chipman decomposition is an effective and practical pretext task for robust biomedical inference from Mueller polarimetry and can pave the way for future work on label efficient Mueller imaging.

</details>


### [177] [Learning a Particle Dynamics Model with Real-world Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23845)
*Chanho Kim,Suhas V. Sumukh,Li Fuxin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel approach for training neural object dynamics models directly from unlabeled real-world videos by using a particle-based model and Gaussian splatting framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional physics simulators are limited by their non-differentiable nature, and existing data-driven world models rely on synthetic training environments due to the difficulty of obtaining perfect state information in real-world settings.

Method: The proposed method involves a particle-based dynamics model compatible with a Gaussian splatting framework, which predicts position and rotation changes of particles derived from Gaussians. The model is trained using rendering supervision from real-world videos, eliminating the need for labeled particle-level data.

Result: The paper introduces a new framework that bypasses the need for synthetic training environments or heuristic subsampling, making it possible to train physics models from real-world video data.

Conclusion: The study provides a method for bridging the sim-to-real gap in physics simulation models by enabling training directly from real-world unlabeled videos, expanding their applicability to real-world scenarios.

Abstract: Data-driven learning approaches for physics simulation, sometimes referred to as world models, have emerged as promising alternatives to traditional physics simulators due to their differentiable nature. Prior work has demonstrated impressive results in predicting the motions of rigid and non-rigid objects in complex scenes involving multiple interacting bodies. However, these models are typically trained in simulated environments because obtaining perfect state information such as complete scene point clouds and point correspondences over time is challenging in real-world settings. This reliance on synthetic data can limit their applicability when the sim-to-real gap is large. In this work, we aim to overcome these limitations by introducing a novel framework for training neural object dynamics models directly from unlabeled real-world videos. Specifically, we propose to learn a particle-based dynamics model compatible with a Gaussian splatting framework, which operates on dense particles derived from Gaussians (i.e., particles with scales and rotations) and predicts their position and rotation changes over time. The model is trained via rendering supervision, enabling learning from real-world videos without requiring particle-level labeled states. Our model operates directly on dense Gaussians without relying on heuristic subsampling anchor points. To enable this study, we also present a real-world dataset consisting of about 500 videos capturing diverse object interactions.

</details>


### [178] [Vision Transformers Need Better Token Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23868)
*Linxiang Su*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper identifies semantic diffusion as a key issue in Vision Transformers (ViTs) for dense prediction tasks and proposes sparse attention with entmax-1.5 to address it, significantly improving semantic segmentation performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While Vision Transformers excel at image-level representation, their patch representations degrade for dense prediction tasks over prolonged training. This study aims to investigate and address this issue.

Method: The paper analyzes dense degradation through semantic diffusion and advocates for selective token interactions rather than removing global context. Sparse attention using entmax-1.5 is introduced to enhance token interaction selectively, preserving global token connectivity.

Result: Using sparse attention with entmax-1.5 increases semantic segmentation performance significantly: VOC mIoU improved from 42.80 to 48.78, ADE20K from 19.85 to 21.97, and Cityscapes from 36.79 to 37.87, while maintaining ImageNet linear probing accuracy.

Conclusion: The findings indicate that selective token interactions, rather than eliminating global context, improve dense ViT representations, providing a simple yet impactful method to enhance their performance in dense prediction tasks.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) can learn strong image-level representations while their patch representations become less effective for dense prediction during prolonged training. We revisit this dense degradation phenomenon and argue that it is not fully explained by high-norm artifacts alone. Instead, we characterize \emph{semantic diffusion}: an optimization shortcut in which global semantic information spreads through patch tokens beyond what is locally justified. Our analysis shows that dense representation quality is not captured by locality alone: shallow features can remain better aligned with foreground regions yet underperform deeper features, and \texttt{[CLS]} features remain complementary for dense prediction. These observations suggest that the goal should not be to remove global context, but to make token interactions more selective. We therefore study sparse attention as a minimal intervention, replacing softmax attention with entmax-1.5 while preserving global token connectivity. On DINOv1 ViT-S/16 trained for 200 epochs on ImageNet-1K, this change preserves ImageNet linear probing accuracy and substantially improves semantic segmentation performance: VOC mIoU increases from 42.80 to 48.78, ADE20K from 19.85 to 21.97, and Cityscapes from 36.79 to 37.87. These results suggest that selective token mixing is a simple and effective bias for improving dense ViT representations.

</details>


### [179] [LaMo: Self-Supervised Latent Motion Priors for Physical Realism in Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23878)
*Bo Jiang,Depu Meng,Yihan Hu,Yichen Xie,Tianshuo Xu,Wei Zhan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces LaMo, a method to enhance motion consistency in video diffusion models without needing external simulators or labeled data, by leveraging motion cues from unlabeled training videos.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of modern video generation models that struggle with physical and motion consistency, which restricts their reliability as world simulators.

Method: The paper proposes LaMo, which implements a latent motion prior with two components: Motion Drift Loss for training and Motion Prior Guidance for sampling, both integrated seamlessly with video diffusion backbones.

Result: LaMo improves motion consistency in video generation, enhancing the performance of CogVideoX and surpassing recent physics-aware methods in specific benchmarks, while maintaining overall generation quality.

Conclusion: Unlabeled video contains valuable motion supervision that can improve physical fidelity in video diffusion models without requiring external supervision or architectural changes.

Abstract: Modern video generators produce visually compelling clips but still struggle with physical and motion consistency, limiting their use as reliable world simulators. Existing remedies often rely on external simulators, teacher models, or curated physics-focused data. We explore a complementary self-supervised direction: extracting motion cues from the unlabeled videos already used to train video diffusion models. We propose LaMo, which formulates a latent motion prior over frame-to-frame latent changes conditioned on the current latent and prompt. This prior is exposed through two lightweight readouts: a macro motion drift used during training as a Motion Drift Loss, and a learned micro motion field used during sampling as Motion Prior Guidance. Both components are plug-and-play with existing video diffusion backbones, requiring no architectural or I/O changes. On VideoPhy and VideoPhy2, LaMo improves CogVideoX backbones and outperforms recent physics-aware baselines that use external supervision. On VBench, it preserves overall generation quality while improving motion-related dimensions. These results suggest that unlabeled video contains useful motion supervision for improving physical fidelity in modern video diffusion models.

</details>


### [180] [PGT: Procedurally Generated Tasks for improving visual grounding in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23883)
*Rim Assouel,Amir Bar,Michal Drozdzal,Adriana Romero-Soriano*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces Procedurally Generated Tasks (PGT), a framework for enhancing fine-grained visual understanding and diagnosing perceptual failures in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the fine-grained understanding capabilities of MLLMs and better diagnose the source of perception failures, which is challenging for current models.

Method: The authors propose PGT, which overlays unambiguous geometric primitives on images to provide additional dense supervision, disentangling visual grounding from semantic priors. They apply this framework to various MLLM architectures and benchmarks.

Result: Using PGT, MLLMs show substantial performance gains, with up to +20% on the What'sUp benchmark and +13.3% on CV-Bench-2D through instruction tuning and additional improvements of up to +8.3% through fine-tuning.

Conclusion: PGT effectively enhances fine-grained perception and proves that many spatial reasoning deficits in MLLMs arise from inadequate supervision rather than architectural limitations, offering a low-cost, diagnostic solution.

Abstract: Despite remarkable progress in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), these models still struggle with fine-grained understanding tasks. In this work, we propose Procedurally Generated Tasks (PGT), a simple data-driven framework that serves a dual purpose: inducing fine-grained visual understanding and acting as a low-cost diagnostic tool to identify the source of perception failures. By overlaying unambiguous geometric primitives on images, PGT generate additional dense supervision that disentangles visual grounding capability from semantic priors. Extensive experiments on relational, quantitative, and 3D/depth understanding benchmarks show that PGT yields remarkable gains across diverse architectures. Instruction tuning MLLMs on LLaVA-v1.5-Instruct augmented with PGT data results in improvements of up to +20% on the What'sUp benchmark and +13.3% on CV-Bench-2D, while maintaining general perception capabilities. Moreover, finetuning state-of-the-art MLLMs on PGT data leads to boosts of up to +5.5% on What'sUp and +8.3% on CV-Bench-2D. These findings demonstrate that PGT effectively address the bottleneck of fine-grained perception, revealing that many spatial reasoning deficits stem from inadequate supervision signals rather than inherent architectural or resolution limitations.

</details>


### [181] [ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23897)
*Beichen Zhang,Yuhong Liu,Jinsong Li,Yuhang Zang,Jiaqi Wang,Dahua Lin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces ETCHR, a reasoning-aware image editor designed to complement multimodal large language models by enhancing their visual reasoning capabilities through image transformations. It fills gaps in off-the-shelf image editors and demonstrates improved performance on five reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the limitations of purely textual reasoning in multimodal large language models, particularly for tasks requiring image-based transformations. Existing methods either suffer from rigid toolkits or noisy image outputs, prompting the need for a more effective and flexible approach.

Method: The authors propose a decoupled system, ETCHR, which is a question-conditioned image editor. It is trained in two stages: (1) supervised fine-tuning on editing trajectories to imitate reasoning, and (2) enhancement with VLM-derived rewards focused on edit correctness and downstream reasoning accuracy.

Result: ETCHR improves reasoning performance across five task families, increasing Pass@1 scores significantly when integrated with different MLLMs like Qwen3-VL-8B, Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite, and Kimi K2.5.

Conclusion: ETCHR effectively bridges the limitations of existing approaches by providing a reasoning-aware image editing system that can seamlessly integrate with various multimodal large language models to enhance reasoning capabilities without additional training.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models have advanced visual reasoning, yet a purely textual chain of thought remains a bottleneck for questions that require fine-grained focus or view transformations. The ''think with images'' paradigm narrows this gap, but existing approaches are either constrained by fixed predefined toolkits or produce noisy intermediate images from unified multimodal methods. We pursue a third option: using a dedicated image editing model and decouple it with an understanding model. However, off-the-shelf image editors fail as reasoning assistants with two complementary gaps: a language-side gap, where editors trained as passive instruction-followers cannot map an abstract question to an appropriate visual transformation, and a generation-side gap, where edit correctness degrades as reasoning depth grows. Guided by this analysis, we introduce ETCHR (Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning), a question-conditioned, reasoning-aware image editor decoupled from the downstream understanding model and trained with a two-stage recipe targeted at the two gaps: Reasoning Imitation via supervised fine-tuning on edit trajectories, followed by Reasoning Enhancement with VLM-derived rewards for edit correctness and downstream reasoning accuracy. Since the editor is decoupled, ETCHR plugs into different open- and closed-source MLLMs in a training-free manner. Across five task families (fine-grained perception, chart understanding, logic reasoning, jigsaw restoration, and 3D understanding), ETCHR raises average Pass@1 from 55.95 to 60.77 (+4.82) with Qwen3-VL-8B, from 65.08 to 70.55 (+5.47) with Gemini-3.1-Flash-Lite, and from 76.55 to 81.16 (+4.61) with the 1T-parameter MoE model Kimi K2.5.

</details>


### [182] [GenRecon: Bridging Generative Priors for Multi-View 3D Scene Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23888)
*Katharina Schmid,Nicolas von Lützow,Jozef Hladký,Angela Dai,Matthias Nießner*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a new method to achieve high-fidelity 3D scene reconstruction using multi-view RGB images and a state-of-the-art generative 3D prior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the completeness and fidelity of 3D scene reconstruction by leveraging strong generative shape models and adapting them to scene-level scales.

Method: The authors generalize the generative shape model Trellis.2 for scene-level reconstruction, using a projection-based conditioning mechanism to transform multi-view imagery into a spatially consistent 3D representation.

Result: The proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art 3D reconstruction techniques by 16% in terms of fidelity and completeness.

Conclusion: This approach effectively combines generative models with multi-view geometry, enabling consistent, scalable, and high-quality 3D reconstructions of indoor scenes.

Abstract: We introduce a new approach to high-fidelity 3D scene reconstruction from multi-view RGB images that tightly couples reconstruction with a strong generative 3D prior. We cast scene reconstruction as conditional 3D generation over a set of spatially-localized, overlapping chunks that together tile the scene, scaling generation to large scene extents. Crucially, we inherit the fidelity and completeness of state-of-the-art generative shape models -- we use Trellis.2 as an example -- which we generalize to the scene level. To this end, we propose a projection-based conditioning mechanism that lifts posed multi-view image features into a coherent 3D representation aligned with the generative model, independent of view ordering and spatially anchored to the scene, yielding high-fidelity, multi-view consistent generated geometry. This enables lifting the strong object-level prior of Trellis.2 to multi-view, scene-scale generation, producing faithful, editable PBR mesh reconstructions of indoor environments. As a result, we obtain high-fidelity results that outperform cutting-edge reconstruction methods by 16%.

</details>


### [183] [HorizonStream: Long-Horizon Attention for Streaming 3D Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23889)
*Chong Cheng,Peilin Tao,Nanjie Yao,Guanzhi Ding,Xianda Chen,Yuansen Du,Xiaoyang Guo,Wei Yin,Weiqiang Ren,Qian Zhang,Zhengqing Chen,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes HorizonStream, a long-horizon Transformer for robust online 3D reconstruction with constant memory and improved performance over long sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing online 3D reconstruction methods struggle with long-sequence issues such as drift, jitter, and collapse due to mismatched temporal modeling strategies.

Method: The method introduces HorizonStream, which uses a formalized evidence influence kernel. Key components include Geometric Linear Attention for multi-timescale propagation, Geometric Local Attention with Spatiotemporal RoPE for reliable 3D matching, and Metric Readout Tokens for recovering stable geometry estimates.

Result: HorizonStream generalizes from short clips to over 10,000 frames with state-of-the-art performance, using constant memory and operating in linear time.

Conclusion: The proposed approach resolves issues in streaming 3D reconstruction by properly modeling temporal and spatial factors, enabling superior performance on long-duration data.

Abstract: Online 3D reconstruction requires estimating camera pose and scene geometry under strict causal and bounded-memory constraints. Existing methods often suffer from drift, jitter, or collapse on long sequences. We trace these failures to a fundamental mismatch. Streaming geometry is inherently temporally heterogeneous, with evidence ranging from short-lived correspondences to persistent global scale. However, current architectures impose uniform and pathological influence patterns. For example, sliding windows enforce hard cutoffs, while ungated recurrence and causal attention cause cache saturation and spike-like attention sinks. To resolve this, we formalize geometric propagation as an \emph{evidence influence kernel} and propose HorizonStream, a long-horizon Transformer that explicitly factorizes this kernel. For the long-range temporal factor, Geometric Linear Attention learns channel-wise decay rates to enable bounded, multi-timescale propagation of geometric evidence. For the short-range spatial factor, Geometric Local Attention with Spatiotemporal RoPE performs reliable 3D matching while suppressing attention sinks. Finally, Metric Readout Tokens recover stable scale and rigid pose directly from the persistent geometric state. Extensive experiments show that HorizonStream, trained on only 48-frame clips, generalizes stably to sequences exceeding 10,000\ frames with constant memory and linear time, achieving state-of-the-art streaming 3D reconstruction performance. Project Page: https://3dagentworld.github.io/horizonstream/

</details>


### [184] [Smart-Insertion-V: Photorealistic Video Insertion via a Closed-Loop Feedback Dual-Stream Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23891)
*Xiao Cao,Yansong Qu,Xiangzhen,Chang,Wen Xiao,Jiakui Hu,Heyuan Li,Jialun Liu,Zhiyong Huang,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces "Smart-Insertion-V," an end-to-end dual-stream framework for mask-free video object insertion, addressing style domain gap challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video object insertion methods struggle to harmonize objects when references and source scenes have significant style differences.

Method: The authors propose a Dual-Stream framework with image and video streams, incorporating a Closed-loop Feedback mechanism, Dual-World-View RoPE, and a Decoupled Guidance Module with a Vision-Language Model.

Result: The method achieves robust object insertion with harmonious style adaptation, as validated by experiments.

Conclusion: Smart-Insertion-V proves effective in seamlessly integrating references into video scenes, and the authors further contribute by offering an open-source dataset for this purpose.

Abstract: Mask-free video object insertion has emerged as a challenging task, requiring harmonious integration of reference objects into source videos. However, existing methods struggle when references exhibit severe stylistic domain gaps with the source scene. To overcome this, we propose \textit{\textbf{Smart-Insertion-V}}, an end-to-end \textbf{Dual-Stream} framework that concurrently conducts video insertion and image style transfer. Within this framework, the image stream synchronously guides the video generation process, while a \textbf{Closed-loop Feedback} mechanism is further incorporated to ensure robust insertion. Inevitably, integrating these diverse conditioning signals results in feature entanglement and style leakage. To tackle this issue, we design \textbf{Dual-World-View RoPE} to distinguish different signals via spatial-temporal offsets without incurring heavy training overhead. Furthermore, to facilitate spatial grounding and stylistic adaptation, we introduce a \textbf{Decoupled Guidance Module} that leverages a Vision-Language Model for semantic reasoning while preserving original temporal guidance with native text encoder. To bridge data gap for harmonious reference insertion task, we propose a data curation pipeline and will release an \textbf{open-source dataset}. Experiments demonstrate that our method can insert objects into plausible positions while achieving the most harmonious results.

</details>


### [185] [From Activation to Causality: Discovery of Causal Visual Representations in the Human Brain](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23895)
*Yuval Golbari,Navve Wasserman,Matias Cosarinsky,Roman Beliy,Aude Oliva,Antonio Torralba,Michal Irani,Tamar Rott Shaham*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BrainCause is an automated framework combining generative methods and brain models to identify and validate brain regions representing visual concepts. It overcomes limitations of purely activation-based approaches by employing causal testing with controlled stimuli and encoding models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: This study addresses the challenge of pinpointing brain regions that truly represent visual concepts, noting the limitations of existing methods that rely heavily on activation maximization, which can conflate correlation with causation.

Method: The authors developed BrainCause, a framework that synthesizes controlled stimuli (including concept images, counterfactual edits, and distractor images), predicts brain responses using an image-to-fMRI encoding model, and performs causal testing to discern true representation regions.

Result: BrainCause successfully validated representations of dozens of visual concepts on both predicted and measured fMRI data, distinguishing true representations from false positives caused by correlated cues.

Conclusion: This research highlights the insufficiency of activation-based methods alone for determining neural representations and demonstrates the utility of causal validation through controlled experiments with BrainCause.

Abstract: Identifying which brain regions represent a visual concept in the human brain is a central challenge in neuroscience. Existing approaches have localized coarse functional regions (e.g., faces, places) through activation maximization, identifying regions that activate strongly for a target concept relative to other concepts. Yet strong activation alone does not establish that a region represents the concept itself, as responses may instead be driven by correlated visual or semantic cues. We introduce BrainCause, an automated framework that combines generative and brain models to synthesize controlled stimuli and validate neural representations through targeted causal testing. Given a query specifying a concept of interest, our framework constructs targeted stimulus sets comprising concept images, counterfactual edits that remove the target concept while preserving other image content, and images with candidate correlated distractors. It then uses an image-to-fMRI encoding model to predict brain responses and searches for representations that respond specifically to the target concept over correlated alternatives. BrainCause returns validated candidate representations and proposes follow-up fMRI experiments to further test or extend its discoveries. Our approach successfully recovers known functional localizations and identifies new candidate representations across dozens of concepts, validated on both predicted and measured fMRI data. Critically, we show that without causal validation, a large fraction of localizations would be false positives, confirming that activation alone is insufficient evidence of representation.

</details>


### [186] [PiD: Fast and High-Resolution Latent Decoding with Pixel Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23902)
*Yifan Lu,Qi Wu,Jay Zhangjie Wu,Zian Wang,Huan Ling,Sanja Fidler,Xuanchi Ren*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces PiD, a Pixel diffusion Decoder that integrates decoding and upsampling via conditional pixel diffusion. It achieves high-resolution image generation quickly and with low memory usage, surpassing traditional methods in speed and fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard latent-to-pixel decoders focus on reconstruction rather than detail synthesis and become computationally expensive at larger scales, necessitating a new approach for expressive and efficient decoding.

Method: PiD employs pixel-space diffusion as a decoding mechanism and uses a sigma-aware adapter for latent conditioning. Additionally, DMD2 distillation reduces inference steps, improving efficiency.

Result: The PiD decoder generates high-resolution images (up to 2048 x 2048 pixels) significantly faster than traditional cascaded super-resolution methods, with superior visual quality and memory efficiency.

Conclusion: PiD offers a unified and efficient approach for latent-to-pixel decoding and high-resolution image generation, making it highly practical for consumer-grade hardware.

Abstract: Most practical high-resolution text-to-image systems, including latent diffusion and autoregressive models, perform generation in a compact latent space, and a decoder maps the generated latents back to pixels. Yet the latent-to-pixel decoder is reconstruction-oriented, optimized to invert the encoder rather than synthesize more details, and becomes increasingly costly at megapixel scale. This drawback calls for a more expressive and efficient decoding paradigm. Motivated by recent progress in scalable pixel-space diffusion, we introduce PiD, a Pixel diffusion Decoder that reformulates latent decoding as conditional pixel diffusion, unifying decoding and upsampling into one generative module. By denoising directly in high-resolution pixel space, PiD synthesizes $4\times$ and even $8\times$ upscaled images with low latency. For latent conditioning, a lightweight sigma-aware adapter injects noise-corrupted latents into the pixel diffusion backbone, enabling PiD to decode partially denoised latents and terminate the latent diffusion process early. To further improve efficiency, we distill the model using DMD2, reducing inference to just 4 steps. PiD applies to both conventional VAE latents and semantic latents (e.g., SigLIP, DINOv2) used in recent RAE-based models. PiD decodes latents of $512 \times 512$ images into $2048 \times 2048$ pixels in under 1 second with 13 GB peak memory on a consumer RTX 5090, and as fast as 210 ms on a GB200 GPU, about $6\times$ faster than cascaded diffusion-based super-resolution pipelines with better visual fidelity.

</details>


### [187] [Geo-Align: Video Generation Alignment via Metric Geometry Reward](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23903)
*Zizun Li,Haoyu Guo,Runzhe Teng,Chunhua Shen,Tong He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces Geo-Align, a Reinforcement Learning-based framework that enhances camera-controlled video re-rendering using a scale-aware reward system and novel data pipeline strategies, achieving superior camera alignment and visual quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional video re-rendering methods rely on supervised learning with synthetic datasets, limiting their generalizability to out-of-distribution real-world videos due to the lack of synchronized, multi-view real-world data.

Method: The Geo-Align framework integrates Reinforcement Learning with a scale-aware perceptual reward mechanism that utilizes a 3D estimator to enforce accurate camera trajectories. It also includes a specifically designed data pipeline for real-world and synthetic data fusion without requiring paired datasets.

Result: Geo-Align achieves higher precision in camera control and enhanced visual fidelity when compared to existing supervised methods, as confirmed by extensive experimental evaluations.

Conclusion: The proposed method successfully addresses the generalization limitations of existing methods, demonstrating its potential for accurate and visually consistent camera-controlled video generation in real-world settings.

Abstract: Camera-controlled video generation has achieved remarkable progress in recent years. However, existing video-to-video re-rendering methods primarily rely on Supervised Fine-Tuning using synthetic datasets. At present, there is an extreme scarcity of synchronized, multi-view real-world video data. Consequently, the prevailing paradigm often exhibits limited generalization when processing out-of-distribution real-world videos, with models struggling to accurately adhere to physical scales and camera trajectories. To bridge this gap, we propose Geo-Align, the first Reinforcement Learning framework specifically designed for camera-controlled video re-rendering. Built upon a pretrained model, we optimize the model through a scale-aware perceptual reward mechanism. Specifically, we introduce a metric 3D estimator to extract precise camera trajectories from generated videos, explicitly penalizing deviations in rotation and translation. Furthermore, we meticulously designed a data pipeline strategy based on real-world conditioning videos and target camera trajectories derived from synthetic data, eliminating the reliance on paired data. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Geo-Align consistently outperforms existing supervised learning baselines in both precise camera controllability and visual fidelity, indicating the effectiveness of our method.

</details>


<div id='cs.DC'></div>

# cs.DC [[Back]](#toc)

### [188] [KPI2KVI: A Multi Agent Workflow for Calculating Key Value Indicators from Service Descriptions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22825)
*Masoud Shokrnezhad,Tarik Taleb,Yan Chen,Qize Guo*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper introduces KPI2KVI, a tool that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to automate the transformation of natural language service descriptions into computed Key Value Indicators (KVIs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Computing KVIs is challenging due to the manual and inconsistent process of identifying KVI categories, defining KPIs, collecting values, and applying calculation logic from unstructured documentation.

Method: KPI2KVI employs a deterministic multi-agent workflow powered by LLMs to perform tasks such as eliciting missing service context, selecting KVI categories, generating KPIs, collecting/estimating KPI values, and calculating traceable KVI outputs.

Result: Simulations demonstrate that KPI2KVI can consistently map service descriptions to KVI intervals, providing transparent narratives and supporting interactive and auditable processes.

Conclusion: KPI2KVI effectively addresses the challenges in computing KVIs from service descriptions, providing a systematic and explainable approach for decision-making and auditing.

Abstract: Key Value Indicators (KVIs) provide a decision oriented view of a service by summarizing how operational performance translates into stakeholder value, risk, and outcomes. However, in many domains KVIs are difficult to compute in practice because they require selecting relevant KVI categories, defining measurable Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), collecting KPI values, and applying consistent calculation logic, all of which is typically performed manually and inconsistently from unstructured service documentation. This paper presents KPI2KVI, a tool that transforms a natural language service description into computed KVI estimates by orchestrating a deterministic multi agent workflow powered by Large Language Models (LLMs) that (i) elicits missing service context, (ii) extracts and finalizes relevant KVI categories from a taxonomy, (iii) generates service specific KPIs with units and descriptions, (iv) collects KPI values through an interactive dialogue and also supports intelligent estimation for KPI values that are unavailable, and (v) computes interval valued KVI outputs (minimum, exact, maximum) with traceable explanations for each KVI code. Simulations with representative service descriptions demonstrate that KPI2KVI consistently produces a complete end to end mapping from description to KVI intervals and provides transparent calculation narratives that support post hoc auditing and interactive advisory queries.

</details>


### [189] [An AI-Driven Framework for Energy-Efficient Environmental Monitoring in Smart Cities Using Edge Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22824)
*Yichen Liu,Imam Akintomiwa Akinlade,Xiaochong Jiang,Wenting Yang,Shiqi Yang*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: This paper proposes an AI-driven, energy-efficient framework for environmental monitoring in smart cities using TinyML-enabled edge devices and adaptive decision-making. It reduces energy consumption and extends sensor lifespan.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address challenges like excessive energy use, redundant data collection, and limited sensor lifespan in large-scale sensor deployments for smart city monitoring.

Method: The paper introduces an AI-powered framework that uses TinyML-enabled edge devices and context-aware decision-making. A utility function is employed to dynamically activate sensors based on factors like environmental conditions, location, and battery lifespan. Hierarchical Edge Intelligence supports city-wide deployment.

Result: The proposed framework showed significant reductions in energy consumption and extended sensor lifespan in a city-scale simulation using real environmental sensor data, outperforming existing methods like static and UCB-based sensing.

Conclusion: The study underscores the value of edge intelligence and adaptive AI techniques, highlighting their role in creating efficient and sustainable environmental monitoring systems for smart cities.

Abstract: Environmental monitoring is a crucial component of the smart city infrastructure. It enables informed decision making which enhances sustainability, public health and urban planning. However, the large-scale deployments of the smart sensors have raised concerns on excessive energy consumption and redundant data collection as well as limited sensor lifespan. To resolve these issues, we present an AI-driven framework for energy-efficient environmental monitoring in smart cities utilizing edge intelligence. Our proposed framework leverages TinyML-enabled edge devices and context-aware adaptive decision-making in order to dynamically activate the sensors based on the spatiotemporal conditions, environmental statistics and energy constraints. The sensors will be dynamically activated based on a utility function that takes in factors such as real-time environmental conditions, sensor location, and remaining battery lifespan. Our framework will reduce unnecessary sensing and communication while maintaining high coverage for monitoring. We introduce a hierarchical Edge Intelligence architecture to support deployments in city-wide scales. We conducted evaluation using a city-scale simulation driven by real multi-sensor environmental traces, which demonstrates that the proposed mechanism significantly reduces energy consumption and extends sensor lifespan when compared to static, periodic, and UCB-based adaptive sensing strategies. The results highlight the potential of edge intelligence and adaptive AI techniques for building sustainable and efficient smart city monitoring systems.

</details>


### [190] [Intercloud: Eventual Consistency for Decentralised Economies via Chilling-Effect Consensus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22830)
*Gregory Magarshak*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: Intercloud is a decentralized economic network that secures private data streams using cryptographic mechanisms without requiring global consensus. It employs innovative ripple deduplication and chilling-effect consensus strategies for security.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind the paper is to design a decentralized system for securely managing private data streams without relying on global consensus mechanisms while ensuring robustness against attacks like double-spending.

Method: Intercloud achieves security through two core mechanisms: (i) ripple deduplication prevents the propagation of execution ripples through the same node multiple times per epoch, and (ii) chilling-effect consensus ensures finality by confirming the absence of conflicts. Additionally, it uses a combination of verifiable random function for watcher assignment and an economic model with local coins and rewards to incentivize honest behavior.

Result: The paper establishes four main results: (1) bounded-time execution ripple termination, (2) sufficiency of about 35 Watchers for double-spending prevention, (3) conditions necessary and sufficient for conflicting finality attestations, and (4) exclusion of Buridan's Principle due to the nature of the consensus mechanism.

Conclusion: Intercloud presents a novel, scalable, and economically rational approach to decentralized data stream security and consensus. Its separation of the coin and content layers ensures both regulatory compliance and privacy.

Abstract: We present Intercloud, a decentralised economic network in which streams of private data are secured by Watcher swarms that observe only cryptographic hashes, never plaintext. Intercloud requires no global consensus beyond a single shared random seed per epoch. Two mechanisms provide security: (i) ripple deduplication via epoch-stamped identifiers, preventing any ripple from propagating through the same node twice per epoch, guaranteeing termination without global coordination; and (ii) chilling-effect consensus, in which a swarm reaches finality by attesting to the absence of conflicting evidence rather than voting between alternatives. Any conflicting attestation automatically yields a self-certifying Proof of Corruption.
  We prove four main results. First, execution ripples terminate in bounded time via the ripple-ID mechanism. Second, a swarm of about 35 Watchers -- assigned by a verifiable random function, independent of total network size -- suffices for double-spending prevention, matching Hoepman's lower bound. Third, two correct clients can hold conflicting finality attestations only if the adversary compromises a supermajority of the assigned swarm or eclipses both clients from all honest nodes; we prove necessity and sufficiency. Fourth, Buridan's Principle does not apply: the consensus question is absence of evidence, not a binary choice on a continuous input.
  We also develop a complete economic model. Local coins are issued and retired by currency streams; security weight tracks value automatically as Intercoin weight adjusts at each epoch shuffle. Junior nodes detect corruption and earn lottery rewards for propagating Proofs of Corruption; vesting makes corruption economically irrational. The coin and content layers are strictly separated: regulators observe weight flows without learning amounts, coin types, identities, or rules.

</details>


### [191] [Monte Cimone v3: Where RISC-V Stands in High-Performance Computing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22831)
*Emanuele Venieri,Simone Manoni,Giacomo Madella,Federico Proverbio,Federico Ficarelli,Luca Benini,Andrea Bartolini*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The Monte Cimone v3 project introduces a RISC-V HPC cluster using the SOPHGO Sophon SG2044 processor, achieving notable energy efficiency and competitive performance compared to mainstream x86 and Arm servers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop and benchmark a high-performance computing (HPC) cluster based on RISC-V architecture, addressing energy efficiency and scalability while evaluating how it compares against established x86 and Arm platforms.

Method: The researchers utilized the SOPHGO Sophon SG2044 processor in the third iteration of the Monte Cimone cluster. They conducted benchmarking using HPL and STREAM along with power measurement, and results were compared against Intel Xeon Platinum (Sapphire Rapids) and NVIDIA Grace CPU Superchip platforms.

Result: MCv3 exhibited more than twice the single-core performance of its predecessor (SG2042), achieved a peak energy efficiency of 3.08GFLOPs/W (10x improvement over MCv1), and demonstrated competitive performance—46% of Intel Sapphire Rapids and 91% of NVIDIA Grace CPU Superchip when normalized on SIMD/Vector length.

Conclusion: The Monte Cimone v3 cluster demonstrates significant advancements in RISC-V-based HPC, offering substantial energy efficiency and competitive performance, making it a promising alternative to traditional x86 and Arm servers for certain use cases.

Abstract: The Monte Cimone project provides a RISC-V testbed for High-Performacne Computing cluster. This paper presents Monte Cimone v3 (MCv3), the third iteration of the Monte Cimone RISC-V HPC cluster, integrating the SOPHGO Sophon SG2044 processor, an evolution of the SG2042 used in MCv2. We characterize MCv3 using HPL and STREAM benchmarks coupled with power measurements, and compare it against two reference platforms: the Intel Xeon Platinum 8480+(Sapphire Rapids) and the NVIDIA Grace CPU Superchip. Our results show that the SG2044 more than doubles single-core performance and improves scalability compared to SG2042. MCv3 achieves an energy efficiency of 3.08GFLOPs/W which improves of 10x w.r.t. MCv1 and is in the range of x86-64 and Arm servers. On pure performance when normalized on the SIMD/Vector length MCv3 on its peak efficiency point (16 cores) achieves 46% performance of Intel Sapphire Rapids server and 91% performance of NVIDIA Grace CPU superchip.

</details>


### [192] [Mathematical Foundations for Peer-to-Peer Lattice Computation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22832)
*Danil Gorinevski*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper presents five propositions on synchronous peer-to-peer computation on grid graphs, addressing diverse bounds, routing efficiencies, and topology enhancements with theoretical underpinnings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To analyze computational efficiency, network routing, and effectiveness of grid graph structures for synchronous peer-to-peer computations under different activation and communication models.

Method: The paper employs mathematical and theoretical approaches, such as algebraic criteria, cluster-size decay, and collective-communication cost modeling, to derive results for computational and structural optimization.

Result: Five propositions introduce results such as lower bounds on work and routing metrics, latency-ratio improvement, schedule-independent reduction semantics, route-length conditional expectations, and enhanced topology with shortcuts collapsing shortest-path lengths.

Conclusion: The results provide theoretical foundations for improving grid graph efficiency in synchronous computations, demonstrating potential for reduced complexity and enhanced scalability with optimized routing and topology.

Abstract: Five propositions on synchronous peer-to-peer computation on a grid graph in $\mathbb{Z}^2$.
  \textbf{Proposition~1} gives three lower bounds: a transport-work bound $\sum_i a_i \ell_i \ge W_1(μ,ν)$, a completion-depth bound $D_{\min} \ge r_μ$ on the support radius, and a compressive-reduction edge bound $|E'| \ge \operatorname{St}_G(\operatorname{supp}(μ)\cup\{x_\star\})$. A negative result: for corner-sink dimension-order routing, the sink-trunk route-load functional has variance $Θ(f(1-f)P^2)$ under i.i.d.\ Bernoulli activation, refuting $O(fP^{3/2})$ concentration.
  \textbf{Proposition~2}: under an $αβγ$ collective-communication cost model and a sparse-activation model, the grid-to-cluster latency ratio improves monotonically as $f_{\mathrm{act}}$ shrinks whenever the cluster's fixed overhead dominates the grid's geometric constant.
  \textbf{Proposition~3}: a sufficient algebraic criterion for schedule-independent reduction semantics is that update rules decompose into a local map and an abelian-monoid merge, as a product-preserving functor from the Lawvere theory of commutative monoids into a hardware-state category. Under an idealised non-interfering scheduler this yields a wall-clock bound; the criterion is PCC-certifiable.
  \textbf{Proposition~4} bounds the conditional expected route length under i.i.d.\ site failure in the subcritical regime $δ< p_c^{\mathrm{site}}(\mathbb{Z}^2)$ by an additive detour term, via Aizenman--Barsky cluster-size decay and a boundary-length bound.
  \textbf{Proposition~5} augments the grid with $k$ long-range Watts--Strogatz shortcuts per node, collapsing typical shortest-path length from $Θ(\sqrt{P})$ to $O(\log P)$; percolation is placed in the mean-field universality class (rigorous for the $1$-D-ring base, conjectural for the $2$-D-grid base).

</details>


### [193] [ObjectCache: Layerwise Object-Storage Retrieval for KV Cache Reuse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22850)
*Yu Zhu,Aditya Dhakal,Yunming Xiao,Dejan Milojicic,Gustavo Alonso*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper introduces ObjectCache, a solution to store KV cache in S3-compatible object storage to address the GPU memory limitation in LLM serving. This approach aims to reduce time to first token (TTFT) while utilizing storage efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation of the paper is to address the increasing memory demands of prefix KV caching in large language model (LLM) serving systems, which exceed available GPU and local DRAM capacities. Current solutions rely on remote DRAM pools, raising operational costs. Thus, the paper seeks to find an alternative storage solution to improve scalability and reduce cost without significantly impacting latency.

Method: The proposed method involves designing ObjectCache, which combines a co-designed storage protocol and transfer schedule. It allows S3-compatible object storage to supply KV cache data in the order it is consumed by GPUs, overlapping data transfer and computation. ObjectCache is prototyped on a high-speed RoCE cluster and various storage systems like Ceph RGW and DAOS.

Result: The results show that for 64K contexts (common in modern systems), ObjectCache adds only 5.6% latency compared to local DRAM. For smaller-context (4K) scenarios, it adds 56-75 ms latency. Under shared bandwidth caps, the scheduler reduces added TTFT by 1.2-1.8x compared to naive bandwidth-sharing strategies.

Conclusion: ObjectCache demonstrates that KV caching for LLM serving can leverage object storage to overcome memory limitations while maintaining low TTFT and efficient use of infrastructure. This approach expands the scalability of LLM systems at reduced costs relative to existing methods relying on remote DRAM pools.

Abstract: Prefix KV caching has become a key mechanism in LLM serving: it reduces time to first token (TTFT) by avoiding redundant computation across requests that share a prefix (i.e., the system prompt). However, the accumulated KV cache is often larger than what GPU memory and local DRAM can hold. To preserve latency, current systems keep the KV cache in remote DRAM pools, increasing serving-cluster size and cost. In this paper, we explore a different approach: storing the KV cache in S3-compatible object storage so that capacity is no longer the constraint, while minimizing the impact on TTFT. We propose ObjectCache, which co-designs the storage protocol and transfer schedule so that the storage server delivers KV cache data in the order the GPU consumes it, overlapping data transfer with compute across concurrent requests. We prototype ObjectCache on a 100 Gbps RoCE cluster with NIXL (an inference library that abstracts storage and memory), Ceph RGW (an Object Gateway for clusters), and DAOS (an open source storage system). For 64K contexts, common in today's systems, ObjectCache adds only 5.6\% latency over local DRAM; for 4K contexts, where less compute is available to mask transfer, ObjectCache adds 56--75\,ms over the optimal local layerwise baseline. Under shared bandwidth caps, our scheduler reduces added TTFT by 1.2--1.8x compared with equal bandwidth sharing.

</details>


### [194] [Budgeted Dynamic Trace Structures for Token-Efficient Sequential Computation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22879)
*Faruk Alpay,Levent Sarioglu*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: This paper introduces budgeted dynamic trace structures (BDTS), a framework for managing trace data within a byte or token limit, with formal invariants, an implementation in Rust, and performance benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The growing complexity of sequential computation requires efficient tools to handle large execution traces containing nested branches, status updates, and summaries under size constraints.

Method: The authors propose BDTS, utilizing techniques like status-filtered reachability, cursor pagination, bounded-cost caches, and summary-plus-suffix compaction, with theoretical guarantees and validated through a Rust implementation.

Result: The prototype implementation demonstrates efficient trace graph building (0.58-2.72 ms), descendant enumeration (0.24-1.42 ms), and significant history compaction (from up to 2.71M tokens to 432-4,120 tokens across tests).

Conclusion: BDTS proves effective at maintaining structured, compact traces under strict resource budgets with the potential for application in diverse computational systems.

Abstract: Sequential computation increasingly produces long traces containing nested branches, status transitions, textual payloads, and compact summaries of earlier execution. This paper introduces budgeted dynamic trace structures (BDTS), a data-structural framework for maintaining rooted trace graphs and append-only histories under an explicit byte or token budget. BDTS combines status-filtered reachability, cursor pagination, soft-capped recency logs, reference-counted observation keys, delta overlays, bounded cost caches, and summary-plus-suffix compaction. We give formal invariants, asymptotic bounds, and an ancillary Rust implementation with reproducible benchmarks. Across synthetic traces with 10,000-40,000 vertices, the prototype builds graphs in 0.58-2.72 ms, enumerates all descendants in 0.24-1.42 ms, and compacts histories of 350k-2.71M approximate tokens to 1,048-4,120 approximate tokens. Tokenizer and forward measurements with three public model targets reduce 3,359-3,360 trace tokens to 432-433 tokens.

</details>


### [195] [Orbax: Distributed Checkpointing with JAX](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23066)
*Colin Gaffney,Shutong Li,Daniel Ng,Anastasia Petrushkina,Niket Kumar,Adam Cogdell,Mridul Sahu,Yaning Liang,Nikhil Bansal,Justin Pan,Angel Mau,Abhishek Agrawal,Marco Berlot,Ruoxin Sang,Kiranbir Sodhia,Rakesh Iyer*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: Orbax is a new checkpointing library for JAX offering both user-friendliness and performance improvements over other solutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: JAX lacks a standardized checkpointing solution, leaving users to manage complexities of distributed systems without much guidance.

Method: Orbax introduces a modular, JAX-native checkpointing library designed to simplify distributed system checkpointing and provide flexibility for model lifecycle management.

Result: Orbax outperforms similar PyTorch checkpointing solutions, achieving up to 3.5x faster saving and 2x faster loading performance.

Conclusion: Orbax bridges a critical gap in JAX's ecosystem by providing an efficient and flexible checkpointing library tailored to its distributed and modular nature.

Abstract: In a landscape of high-performance distributed ML systems, JAX has emerged as a framework of choice. However, JAX's modular design philosophy leaves it without a standardized checkpointing solution. In this paper, we introduce Orbax, a modular, JAX-native checkpointing library that abstracts the complexities of distributed accelerator systems while also providing flexibility for user-friendly checkpoint manipulations throughout the ML model lifecycle. We demonstrate performance exceeding comparable PyTorch competitors by up to 3.5$\times$ for saving and 2$\times$ for loading. The library is available at https://github.com/google/orbax.

</details>


### [196] [XWind: A Cross-site Router for Large Language Model Inference Serving at Renewable Energy Farms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23348)
*Tella Rajashekhar Reddy,Atharva Deshmukh,Liangcheng Yu,Chaojie Zhang,Mike Shepperd,Rohan Gandhi,Anjaly Parayil,Srinivasan Iyengar,Ajay Manchepalli,Debopam Bhattacherjee*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: AI Greenferencing is a proposed model that deploys modular AI compute infrastructure close to renewable energy sources to boost AI capabilities, utilize renewable energy effectively, and reduce grid strain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rapidly growing energy demands of AI systems are putting immense pressure on ailing power grids. While renewable energy sources are abundant, they are often not geographically matched with demand. There is a need for innovative solutions to leverage renewable energy efficiently while sustaining the growth of AI workloads.

Method: The paper introduces AI Greenferencing, which places modular AI compute systems near renewable wind energy sources to benefit from local power generation. Additionally, the researchers developed XWind, an AI inference router that uses real-time signals like latency and resource utilization to dynamically manage workloads across distributed sites.

Result: The feasibility analysis identified 890+ GW of wind energy potential close to existing Azure data centers. The XWind inference router, tested on a 64-GPU testbed, achieved significant efficiency improvements, reducing end-to-end latency by up to 52% compared to other approaches and up to 98% compared to traditional methods.

Conclusion: AI Greenferencing and the XWind system pave the way for AI systems to scale sustainably by integrating modular compute with renewable energy sources. This approach has the potential to alleviate grid constraints while maintaining or improving operational efficiency, marking a significant step toward sustainable AI infrastructure.

Abstract: AI power demand is growing at an unprecedented rate while power grids are often ailing and struggle to keep up. Grid expansion comes with high capital expenditure and long-distance transmission losses, yet there is abundant renewable energy at the source, just not matched to demand.
  This paper proposes a complementary AI infrastructure deployment model, AI Greenferencing, that brings modular AI compute to renewable energy sources, focusing on wind, allowing AI footprint expansion, generating local behind-the-meter demand for renewable sites, and helping ease the growing strain on power utilities. Our feasibility analysis shows that 890+ GW of wind capacity lies within 50 ms network round trip time of Azure data centers, and that site-wise right-sizing combined with spatial complementarity of wind energy keeps aggregate fleet utilization on par with traditional deployments.
  To serve inference requests under variable wind power, we build XWind, a lightweight, reactive, and workload-agnostic AI inference router that uses only real-time signals: inference latency, KV-cache utilization, and queue depth, to dynamically configure sites and distribute requests. Evaluated on a real 64-GPU A100 testbed emulating three wind-powered sites with Azure production traces, XWind reduces P99 end-to-end latency by up to 52% over the strongest contender (also our idea) and by up to 98% over baselines such as power-capping and GPU idling, with consistent gains across workload types, load levels, and GPU generations.

</details>


### [197] [AlignedServe: Orchestrating Prefix-aware Batching to Build a High-throughput and Computing-efficient LLM Serving System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23389)
*Fengyao Bai,Hongbin Zhang,Zhitao Chen,Jiangsu Du,Zhiguang Chen,Yutong Lu*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: AlignedServe is a serving framework for large language models (LLMs) that uses prefix-aware batching to optimize iteration-level decoding and reduce inefficiencies caused by differing KV-cache lengths among tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current serving frameworks for LLMs struggle with iteration-level inefficiencies in decoding due to tokens having KV caches of varying lengths, causing delays in the next iteration.

Method: AlignedServe implements prefix-aware batching to group requests with similar KV-cache lengths, utilizes large CPU memory to maintain in-flight requests for efficient batching, adopts a batch-level scheduling policy, and introduces a GPU-Prefetch-For-GPU approach to cut down CPU-to-GPU transfer latency.

Result: AlignedServe achieves up to 1.98x improvement in decoding throughput and up to 7.4x reduction in latency compared to state-of-the-art systems.

Conclusion: The proposed techniques in AlignedServe significantly optimize LLM inference serving, addressing iteration-level inefficiencies and enhancing both throughput and latency for various workloads.

Abstract: High-throughput inference serving is essential for applications built on large language models (LLMs). Existing serving frameworks reduce request-level and batch-level bubbles through batching and scheduling, but often overlook bubbles within each decode iteration. Tokens generated in the same iteration may incur different costs because they depend on KV caches of different lengths; tokens with long KV caches can become bottlenecks and delay the next iteration. We propose AlignedServe, an LLM serving framework built around prefix-aware batching. It groups requests with similar KV-cache lengths into the same batch to reduce iteration-level bubbles. To support this policy efficiently, AlignedServe uses large CPU memory to maintain sufficient in-flight requests for batching and applies a batch-level scheduling policy to reduce batch-level bubbles. It also introduces a GPU-Prefetch-For-GPU architecture, where one GPU prefetches KV cache for another to reduce CPU-to-GPU transfer latency. Experiments on synthetic and application workloads show that AlignedServe improves decoding throughput by up to 1.98 times and reduces latency by up to 7.4 times over state-of-the-art systems.

</details>


### [198] [Multi-Round Visibility: A Post-Consensus Ordering Layer for DAG-Based BFT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23432)
*Pengkun Ren,Dong Hai,Nasrin Sohrabi,Zahir Tari*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper proposes Multi-Round Visibility (MRV), a structural ordering layer for DAG-based BFT protocols, offering fair post-consensus ordering without burdening the consensus path.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DAG-based BFT protocols achieve high throughput but face issues with ambiguous ordering at the execution boundary due to concurrency, which makes ordering dependent on traversal rules or tie-breaking rather than verifiable precedence.

Method: MRV reinterprets the committed DAG structure to derive ordering evidence from authenticated metadata, accumulates this evidence within a bounded horizon, and resolves ambiguities through deterministic graph completion when necessary, all without adding overhead to the consensus path.

Result: The prototype of MRV implemented on a Narwhal/Tusk-based system demonstrates that the approach maintains high throughput across various fault settings and replica counts while ensuring structural ordering after consensus.

Conclusion: MRV enables fair and robust structural ordering for DAG-based BFT systems without increasing the load on the consensus protocol, preserving its high throughput efficiency.

Abstract: Directed acyclic graph (DAG)-based Byzantine Fault-Tolerant (BFT) protocols achieve high throughput by decoupling dissemination from agreement and allowing many vertices to be committed concurrently. This same concurrency, however, weakens ordering evidence at the execution boundary: once units are committed in a shared DAG frontier, their final linearization is driven by traversal or deterministic tie-breaking rather than verifiable structural precedence. Prior fair-ordering designs address ambiguity by collecting or reconstructing transaction-level ordering evidence within the consensus workflow. While effective, this couples ordering with agreement and places ordering logic on the critical path. This paper presents Multi-Round Visibility (MRV), a post-consensus structural ordering layer for DAG-based BFT that reinterprets the committed DAG as an ordering evidence substrate. Committed vertices inherently carry authenticated creator, round, and ancestry metadata, enabling replicas to derive multi-round structural visibility without extra consensus-path messages. MRV accumulates this visibility within a bounded evidence horizon, compares concurrently committed atomic units of fairness (AUFs) after they coexist in the DAG, and derives precedence constraints from Byzantine-robust visibility advantages. When the DAG lacks such constraints, MRV exposes and resolves the remaining ambiguity through deterministic graph completion rather than hiding it inside traversal rules. We implement MRV on a Narwhal/Tusk-based prototype. Evaluation across 5-50 replicas under various fault settings shows MRV preserves the high-throughput regime of the DAG-BFT stack, proving it provides post-consensus structural ordering without burdening the consensus-critical path.

</details>


### [199] [Multi-Factor Trust-Driven Secure Communication Model for Cloud-Based Digital Twins](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23566)
*Deepika Saxena,Ashutosh Kumar Singh*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper introduces MT-SeCom, a multi-factor trust framework for secure communication in cloud-based Digital Twin (DT) platforms, addressing client trust, threat detection, and communication resilience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the critical challenge of ensuring secure and trustworthy communication in cloud-based DT platforms, considering diverse client behaviors and evolving threats.

Method: Proposes the MT-SeCom framework, which operates through four phases: trust monitoring, trust evaluation, client classification using transformers, and communication management.

Result: The framework enhances threat detection accuracy by 18.7% and reduces anomaly occurrences by 24.3%, showing significant improvements compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: MT-SeCom is robust, scalable, and effectively enhances secure communication and resilience in heterogeneous DT-based cloud environments.

Abstract: Cloud-based Digital Twin (DT) platforms enable real-time monitoring, simulation, and collaborative decision-making across distributed clients. However, ensuring secure and trustworthy communication remains a critical challenge due to heterogeneous client behavior, resource contention, and evolving adversarial threats. This paper proposes the Multi-Factor Trust-Driven Secure Communication (MT-SeCom) framework to enforce resilient and intelligent collaboration in DT-enabled cloud environments. MT-SeCom operates through four coordinated phases: (i) Multi-Factor Trust Monitoring, capturing temporal, contextual, and federated trust signals; (ii) Adaptive Trust Evaluation, adjusting trust weights based on network dynamics and threat intensity; (iii) Transformer-Based Trusted Client Classification, combining anomaly detection with supervised learning to accurately identify malicious or unreliable nodes; and (iv) Resilient Communication Management, optimizing routing, isolating compromised clients, and ensuring service continuity. A real-world testbed and comprehensive experiments demonstrate that MT-SeCom significantly enhances secure communication, mitigates cascading adversarial effects, and maintains high resilience under fluctuating attack conditions. MT-SeCom achieves an average 18.7% improvement in threat detection accuracy and a 24.3% reduction in anomaly occurrences compared to existing methods, confirming its robustness, scalability, and practical suitability for heterogeneous cloud-based DT ecosystems.

</details>


### [200] [Herring: Parallel Batch-Order-Fairness on DAG-based Blockchain Consensus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23648)
*Marko Putnik,Jérémie Decouchant*

Main category: cs.DC

TL;DR: The paper introduces Herring, a novel DAG BFT protocol for fair transaction ordering that achieves significantly higher throughput and lower latency than existing solutions, while addressing liveness vulnerabilities in prior protocols.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decentralized finance transaction ordering is vulnerable to Maximal Extractable Value (MEV) attacks due to lack of constraints on order selection in BFT protocols. Existing fairness solutions compromise heavily on performance or face vulnerabilities.

Method: Herring employs post-consensus graph construction with parallelized graph processes and integrates fairness within a multi-proposer DAG structure. It also resolves liveness vulnerabilities found in prior solutions through explicit edge resolution and improved reliability.

Result: Herring demonstrates scalability close to Narwhal  Tusk (10,000 tx/s) and outperforms competitors by achieving 90% higher throughput than FairDAG-RL and 100% higher than DoD-W with reduced latency at saturation.

Conclusion: Herring proves that fair transaction ordering can be achieved efficiently in terms of performance and scalability, addressing critical vulnerabilities that impede liveness in earlier solutions.

Abstract: Transaction ordering attacks extract billions of dollars annually from decentralized finance users in the form of Maximal Extractable Value (MEV). Byzantine Fault-Tolerant (BFT) consensus protocols guarantee total order but place no constraint on how that order is chosen, leaving the door open for adversarial reordering. Batch-order-fairness (batch-OF) protocols close this gap, but existing designs pay a steep performance price for this guarantee. Leader-based protocols such as Themis concentrate all fairness decisions at a single replica, while recent DAG-based proposals FairDAG and DAG of DAGs (DoD) force their fairness layer into strictly serial execution despite running on multi-proposer DAGs.
  We present Herring, the first $γ$-batch-OF DAG BFT protocol whose fairness layer parallelizes the dominant graph construction cost across committed subdags. Herring combines post-consensus graph construction with explicit missing edge resolution piggybacked on the DAG's reliable broadcast layer, a pairing that turns fair ordering from a per-round serial bottleneck into a CPU-bound task. We also uncover previously unreported liveness vulnerabilities in both FairDAG-RL and DoD that a malicious client can trigger to halt the fairness layer indefinitely, and propose patches that we integrate into our reimplementations.
